module: Module 2: In Search of a Universal Theory
content: So in the previous subsection we looked at Marx's ideas of how capitalism is going to be destroyed and why he thinks that. And for us as social theorist, the takeaways from that is this idea of how the different modes of production, how they can change society. The other takeaway for us from these subsections is to look at what are the different classes of society and are we ensuring as a society that each of the classes have a way of improving themselves and there is a sort of a fairness to it because otherwise there is this revolutionary aspect which Marx talks about. Now in this last subsection we will continue with Marx, who discusses well what, what does Marx mean by communism? And it's interesting for us to see, because many of the things that he says are communism, actually, most countries have that today. And especially if you look at the Western European countries if you go through the list of points that Marx makes most of them you know we all consider to be things that society should have. So let's look at what Marx actually says. So what are the aims of the communist? According to Marx, in the national struggles of the proletariats of different countries, communists point out and bring to the front the common interests of the entire proletariat, independent of every nationality. So that's the aim of communists, according to Marx. To forget about all these national I am you know, German, I am whatever, Chinese but to say proletariats, you are all proletariats. You are all fighting a common enemy. So the immediate aim is a formation of the proletariat into a class and then Marx says, and this is the ideolog in Marx he says that, 'After that the aim is to overthrow bourgeoisie supremacy and the conquest of political power by the proletariat'. So, how do you come up with this? How do you do this? You have to abolish all private property. But specifically what Marx is saying is that you have to abolish bourgeoisie private property. Because according to Marx, capital is just surplus accumulated labor, so it should not belong to a few capitalists. So that is what he wants to abolish. So the aim of communism is to do away with the miserable character of appropriation where laborers live only to increase capital. Instead, the aim of communism is to ensure that accumulated labor that is capital must be a means to widen, to enrich, to promote the existence of the laborer. So instead of laborers living to increase capital, capital should be a means to widen, enrich, and promote the existence of the labourer. So then he spends a lot of time sort of answering the objections of these imaginary bourgeoisie whom he's arguing with. So he one of the objections is how can you abolish private property? So Marx's response is, well, already 9/10th of the population has no private property. They don't have capital. They're living day to day. They work, they earn, they eat. If they don't work, they don't eat. So all communism is doing is that it deprives any one of the right to subjugate that labor of others in the form of capital. So another objection is losing freedom, but Marx says, but what is freedom to buy and sell? Well, only the bourgeoisie who have appropriated capital and hence have capital who have appropriated labour and hence have capital. They're the only ones who have this freedom currently. Another objection, we lose our culture. But Marx says, well, the current culture law are all for the benefit of the bourgeoisie. The culture has to be changed because it has to be for the benefit of everyone. On laziness, so another objection is that if private property is abolished and all motivation to work will disappear and society will degenerate into laziness if this way, so then society should have gone to the dogs long time back. Because according to Marx, the bourgeoisie, the capitalists are people who do nothing but get. So those of its members who work acquire nothing, and those who acquire anything do not work, that's a quote from Marx. Another objection on nationality. The communists will come and remove all national nationality. Everybody will be just, you know, the same, it'll be like a one world. So Marx says well, bourgeoisie development is already causing these national divisions to just be less, less and less important because everybody is becoming more and more similar. All that the proprietary victory will do, will, is that it'll accelerate this process on the family. So the objection is that well the family structure will be destroyed. But Marx says, well, the proletariats children are even now working because they have to work for the family to survive. Now, all that communism will do is to stop the exploitation of children. On education, another concern is that now you'll have social education. Everybody will have to have the same education. And now that, that will mean that a parents values will not be passed on to the children. Then Marx says, but today also, the value system that we have is based on the economic conditions, and that has to change, and that is why social education is anyway happening. So Marx says, man's ideas, views, and conceptions in one word, man's consciousness changes with every change in the conditions of his material existence, in his social relations, and in his social life. So the ruling ideas of each age have ever been the ideas of its ruling class. So, you know, now, you know, Marx, we have known Marx for a long time. So even at the dinner table, we might discuss these and say that, oh, history is written by the ruling class. The some of these are coming from Marx's work. And so before going through this clip, I hope you have done your homework and watched. The 2 videos one is off from the Gladiator, the movie Gladiator. And if you haven't seen the movie, it's about this you know, this warrior who has this speech where he basically, he is showing his loyalty to the rightful king and he's willing to give up his life, who he's willing to do anything to play out. That, those ideas of loyalty and serving the rightful king, that's sort of the medieval idea. Those were the medieval value systems. There's another clip which hopefully you have watched, which is of God in Gecko, where he makes this strong speech about how greed is good. So that is the capitalist value system, meritocracy, self-interest. There's no nobility, we don't have an aristocracy anymore. It's all about self-interest. It's all about merit. And so these 2 clips are meant to kind of give you a sense of how Marx explains to us this idea of base and superstructure as the modes of production change, the ideas and the value systems law, everything changes. So the what is the outcome according to Marx? What will communism bring? It is 10 key measures and you know, if you have time go through the original manifesto, but basically he's talking about a very heavy progressive income tax, the abolishment of inheritance or an inheritance tax. Free public education, abolishing of child labor, abolishing of the distinction between town and country, centralization of credit, transportation and factories in the hands of the state via nationalized banks and industries, and an equitable distribution of the population across the land. So key concepts that I want you to take away from this subsection on Marx. One is the idea of base and superstructure. Base is the economic foundation of society. So modes of production, instruments of production. Superstructure is everything built on top of that base, and that includes everything from education, value systems, literature, art, law, political system, all of that. And then the idea of historical materialism which is this idea of how we went from primitive society to slave society, to feudalism, to capitalism. And according to Marx, we will finally end with communism, which is an ideal society. But forgetting about that bit, what I want you to take away is this idea of historical materialism where history evolves because there is a change in the material conditions, the economic conditions, the modes of production. So those are the 2 key concepts that I want you to take away with you based in superstructure, historical materialism. And now I want you to apply these concepts to our own lives. So to do that, I have 2 exercises for you. One is I want you to look at our own history, Indian history. The transition, globalization, going from colonialism to an independent country, rural to urban value systems. I want you to think about any of these changes. There are so many, such changes. I want you to think about any of these changes and reflect on how Marx's concepts, historical materialism, and base in superstructure can be used to explain that change. Any social change is complex and can be understood from multiple perspectives. And in this course we'll look at multiple perspectives. Marx is only one among the many perspectives that we will look at, but as part of the access the session on Marx, I want you to apply Marx's ideas to any of these historical changes that we ourselves have seen in our country and analyze it from that perspective. So that's one. Second, I want you to look at the future. It seems clear that AI, robotics, all of this will completely change the mode of production. I want you to think about how this might change the relations of society. Might it lead to even greater concentration of capital where the middle class totally disappears and you just have two extremes, very poor and very rich because the capital needed now is even bigger or maybe there's some other change. Maybe it equalizes the world. Maybe it means that each of us work only 2 hours a day or only 2 days a week, and we spend the rest of our time on improving ourselves. Hobbies, leisure activities, spending time with family, focusing on our health and wellbeing. Maybe that's the change. I want you to think about how this change in the material conditions of production might change things like art, literature, politics, value systems. So with that, I will end the section on Marx and I hope you will do these 2 exercises that you have been given to look at the past and also to look at the future.
